title: Source lineage for CME00050
summary: CME00050 is the repository XML representation of the title page and printed pp. 1–136 of George H. McKnight's 1901 re-edition of EETS OS 14, a collection of three independent Middle English works first edited by J. Rawson Lumby in 1866. McKnight presents King Horn from three manuscripts, Floris and Blancheflour from three manuscripts, and The Assumption of the Virgin in a composite Cambridge-plus-Harley stream beside Additional 10036. Complete provider-published whole-object image bundles are cached for CUL Gg.4.27(2) and Bodleian Laud Misc. 108 with exact work mappings. No public whole-object image download was found for the five British Library codices; their request routes specify the entire present physical object and require foliation confirmation before final work mapping.
review_notes: The collection, its three independent works, the nine XML witness segments, the eight logical edited streams, the seven physical manuscripts, and the printed and digital carriers are distinct units.
Complete manuscript-image coverage means the complete provider-published canvas inventory, not a claim that unexposed spine, edge, or preservation-master views exist.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00050
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion and publication workflow, supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID are all CME00050.
TEMPHEAD records a compact 19 August 2016 review entry, not the medieval works' creation or the original electronic keying date.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00050
entities.description: Public electronic text encoding the 1901 edition title page and main medieval texts on printed pp. 1–136. It omits the preface, introduction, notes, and glossary, although a terminal PB points to p. 137.

entities.label: McKnight's 1901 re-edition of King Horn, Floriz and Blauncheflur, and The Assumption of Our Lady
entities.description: The direct printed source of CME00050. McKnight re-edited Lumby's 1866 EETS collection from the manuscripts, with introduction, notes, and glossary. The volume presents three independent works and does not provide complete textual material for every surviving Floris or Assumption witness.

bibliographic.citation: George H. McKnight, ed., King Horn, Floriz and Blauncheflur, The Assumption of Our Lady, first edited by J. Rawson Lumby, EETS Original Series 14 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society, 1901).
bibliographic.title: King Horn, Floriz and Blauncheflur, The Assumption of Our Lady
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 14
bibliographic.publisher: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society

physical_description.extent: lviii preliminary pages and 171 numbered pages
notes: Contents: preface and introduction pp. v–lviii; King Horn pp. 1–69; Floris pp. 71–110; Assumption pp. 111–136; notes pp. 137–154; glossary pp. 155–171.

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of McKnight's 1901 EETS volume
entities.description: A 240-page PDF access scan retaining the provider's complete exposed bound-volume sequence. The source-edition title page is PDF page 7; printed pp. 1–136 are PDF pages 65–200; PDF page 201 begins the omitted Notes.

entities.label: Lumby's 1866 first EETS edition of King Horn and fragments of Floris and the Assumption
entities.description: The first publication of the three-work EETS collection. Lumby prints CUL Gg.4.27(2) for all three works, Cotton Vitellius D III for Floris, and Additional 10036 for the Assumption; only short Laud and Harley King Horn specimens appear in the preface.

bibliographic.citation: J. Rawson Lumby, ed., King Horn: with Fragments of Floriz and Blauncheflur, and of the Assumption of Our Lady, EETS Original Series 14 (London: Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society, 1866).
bibliographic.title: King Horn: with Fragments of Floriz and Blauncheflur, and of the Assumption of Our Lady
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 14
bibliographic.publisher: Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Lumby's 1866 EETS volume
entities.description: Complete access PDF retained as evidence for the first edition and its manuscript-source claims.

entities.label: Oxford University Press 1962 reprint of McKnight's 1901 edition
entities.description: A reprint of the 1901 McKnight text, identified as the source edition in Project Gutenberg's transcription.

bibliographic.citation: George H. McKnight, ed., King Horn, Floriz and Blauncheflur, The Assumption of Our Lady (Oxford University Press, 1962 reprint of the 1901 edition).
bibliographic.title: King Horn, Floriz and Blauncheflur, The Assumption of Our Lady

entities.label: Project Gutenberg eBook 42713
entities.description: A complete plain-text transcription of the 1962 reprint, released 15 May 2013. Its production note identifies Louise Hope, David Starner, Jack McJiggins, and the Online Distributed Proofreading Team; braces represent source-edition italics used for editorial expansions.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library, MS Gg.4.27(2)
entities.description: A separate bound fourteen-leaf parchment quire containing Floris and Blancheflour on ff. 1r–5v, King Horn on ff. 6r–13r, and the opening 240 lines of the Assumption on ff. 13v–14v. The manuscript is incomplete at both ends; no other part of its former larger codex is known to survive.

date_statements.label: Manuscript production
date_statements.value: First quarter of the fourteenth century

physical_description.extent: i + 14 + i leaves; one quire, separately bound
notes: The same Anglicana hand writes all three works; the current binding dates to 1983.

entities.label: Cambridge Digital Library facsimile of MS Gg.4.27(2)
entities.description: A forty-canvas full-color digital object exposing the provider's complete sequence: covers, inner covers, flyleaves, all fourteen manuscript leaves, and rear surfaces. No separate spine or edge views are published.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation manifest and JPEG page images

entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108
entities.description: A parchment manuscript dated 1301–1325 containing the Laud version of King Horn at ff. 219va–228rb among extensive adjacent contents.

physical_description.extent: 239 foliated leaves plus covers, endleaves, and fragments represented in 496 digital canvases

entities.label: Digital Bodleian facsimile of MS Laud Misc. 108
entities.description: A 496-canvas full-color digital object exposing the complete provider-published codex sequence from upper cover through lower cover. No separate spine or edge views are published.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation manifest and JPEG page images

entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 2253
entities.description: A well-known multilingual manuscript associated with Ludlow and dated c. 1330–1340; King Horn occupies ff. 83r–92v.

physical_description.extent: ff. 1* + 142, with three front and two back flyleaves
notes: Current cataloging associates the relevant portion with the West Midlands and Ludlow.

entities.label: British Library, Egerton MS 2862
entities.description: A late-fourteenth- or c. 1400 parchment manuscript associated with Suffolk. Floris and Blancheflour occupies ff. 98r–111r and begins imperfectly.

entities.label: British Library, Cotton MS Vitellius D III
entities.description: A twenty-six-leaf parchment codex damaged in the 1731 Cotton Library fire. The badly burnt and shrunken Floris and Blancheflour fragments occupy ff. 6r–8v.

notes: The Floris component is ff. 6–8; the codex was rebound in 1970.

entities.label: British Library, Harley MS 2382
entities.description: A late-fifteenth-century paper manuscript. The parent catalogue contents place The Assumption of Our Lady at ff. 75r–86r; McKnight uses the continuation corresponding to lines 241–715, supported by edition milestones as ff. 78v–86r. A component child record instead says ff. 124–126 and is retained as a conflict, not as the work locator.

holding.notes: The parent record is controlling for the present whole-object description; its exact contents and McKnight's milestones outweigh the contradictory child locus pending BL clarification.

physical_description.extent: 129 leaves plus flyleaves according to the parent record

notes: Do not use the child record's ff. 124–126 claim for work mapping unless the British Library reconciles it against the parent contents and supplied images.

entities.label: British Library, Additional MS 10036
entities.description: A fifteenth-century parchment collection of religious works. McKnight's full parallel Assumption text is marked from ff. 62r–80r.

notes: McKnight describes a small volume of about one hundred folios; the current catalog record should control any whole-object order extent.

entities.label: British Library digitisation and imaging service
entities.description: Current service, order form, terms, and 2025/26 rate card for requesting manuscript photography. Manuscript orders may require quotation and special handling; supplied images remain subject to written reuse permissions and any rights identified by the Library.

access.access_method: British Library digitisation service and online order form
access.format: Service information, order form, terms, and PDF rate card

local_copies.label: Complete British Library digitisation rate card 2025/26

work_portion.label: Rates relevant to whole-manuscript photography requests
locators: scanning and photographic reproduction price tables

notes: The 2025/26 card lists standard scan JPEG charges of £15.25 for the first page and £0.58 for each subsequent page, photographic TIFF charges of £27.70 for the first image and £7.25 for each subsequent image, and complex photography at £58.19 per image.

Manuscripts normally require photography rather than ordinary scanning unless an acceptable surrogate exists; specify color and both recto and verso. Published rates are indicative because complete codices and fragile or difficult material may be quoted outside standard pricing.

Identify King Horn separately as provisional target ff. 83r–92v, and confirm that mapping against the supplied foliation before associating image files with the work.
The service may quote large, fragile, or difficult orders separately; reproduction or publication of supplied images requires written permission under the imaging terms.

rights.basis: The current U-M item search record applies No Copyright – United States; the institutional site terms still apply to service use.

rights.component: 1901 printed edition text and editorial matter
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1901 and is public domain in the United States; this does not determine rights in later scan files or provider packaging.

rights.component: Internet Archive PDF scan and item packaging
rights.basis: The underlying 1901 edition is public domain in the United States, but the item metadata exposes no item-specific open license for the scan images or package.

rights.component: 1866 printed edition text and editorial matter
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1866 and is public domain in the United States; this does not determine rights in later scan files or provider packaging.

rights.component: Internet Archive PDF scan and item packaging
rights.basis: The underlying 1866 edition is public domain in the United States, but the item metadata exposes no item-specific open license for the scan images or package.

rights.component: 1962 reprint typography and any independently protectable matter
rights.basis: Project Gutenberg asserts its own eBook is public domain in the United States, but that notice is not an item-specific license for reproducing a 1962 OUP facsimile or its typography in every jurisdiction.

The eBook header states that this eBook is for the use of anyone in the United States and is not protected by U.S. copyright law, while warning users outside the United States to check local law and preserving the Project Gutenberg trademark/license terms.

rights.component: CUDL images made available for download and cached IIIF JPEGs
rights.basis: The CUDL TEI metadata licenses downloadable images CC BY-NC 4.0 and separately describes zoom-view images as all rights reserved.
notes: The noncommercial restriction is incompatible with for-profit print-on-demand use without separate permission.

rights.basis: The CUDL TEI metadata declares metadata CC BY-NC-ND 4.0.

rights.component: Digital Bodleian manuscript photographs and cached IIIF JPEGs
rights.basis: The IIIF manifest attributes the photography to the Bodleian and applies CC BY-NC 4.0; Digital Bodleian terms govern reuse and commercial licensing.
notes: Digital Bodleian permits specified noncommercial academic uses, but commercial or for-profit print-on-demand use requires separate permission and may incur fees.

rights.basis: The imaging terms state that supplied material may be protected and may not be reproduced, including online or in publications, without written permission; applicable fees and third-party rights are item-specific.
The imaging terms require written permission before reproducing supplied material and leave copyright and fees item-specific.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Variant manuscript versions of three independent works
editorial_practices.representation: Parallel page streams, with complete King Horn witnesses and selected Floris and Assumption witnesses
editorial_practices.description: McKnight aligns three King Horn witnesses, three Floris witnesses, and two Assumption streams. The left Assumption stream joins a Cambridge opening fragment to a Harley continuation; Additional 10036 occupies the other stream.

The Project Gutenberg transcription explicitly identifies source-edition italics in the primary texts as editorial expansions and renders them in braces. The manuscript facsimiles should be consulted to distinguish source abbreviation marks from editorial expansion.
editorial_practices.scope: Medieval text streams in all three works

editorial_practices.source_feature: Complete McKnight volume of lviii + 171 pages
editorial_practices.representation: Title page and printed pp. 1–136, with a terminal PB reference to p. 137
editorial_practices.description: The U-M encoding omits the preface, introduction, notes, and glossary while retaining the medieval texts and their page-image reference milestones.

editorial_practices.representation: Nine sequential DIV2 segments with shared PB/image references
editorial_practices.description: The XML serializes visually parallel columns as separate divisions. In particular, it splits the composite Cambridge-Harley Assumption stream into two DIV2 units; consumers must not mistake the serialization for independent authored works or a simple reading order.

open_questions.question: Can complete present-object color photography be obtained for all five British Library codices, and what surfaces, handling limits, total costs, and reuse permissions will apply?
next_steps: Submit one exact whole-codex request per shelfmark, explicitly including covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, all flyleaves, every recto and verso including blanks, adjacent works, binding fragments, and edges where safe.
Require the Library to identify any omitted or unphotographable surfaces, return current foliation metadata, quote the entire order, and state repository and publication permissions in writing.

next_steps: Ask the British Library for a catalog correction or explanation before final mapping.

open_questions.question: How should the current British Library fourteenth-century date for the Cotton Floris component be reconciled with scholarly witness dates near the late thirteenth century?
next_steps: Preserve both attributed date claims and consult recent codicological work before normalizing a narrower witness date.

open_questions.question: Was the U-M item keyed directly from a 1901 impression or from the 1962 reprint of the 1901 text?
next_steps: Compare typographic accidents across both impressions or request U-M production documentation; retain McKnight 1901 as the textual edition layer either way.
